stem: In a university admissions study comparing Department A vs. Department B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher acceptance rate (70.6% vs 56.2%). However, when stratified by applicant pool competitiveness: In less competitive applicants (n_X=40, n_Y=150): Treatment X achieves 87% vs Treatment Y's 77%. In highly competitive applicants (n_X=120, n_Y=30): Treatment X achieves 46% vs Treatment Y's 38%. The true population proportion of less competitive applicants is uncertain, estimated between 43% and 54%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to highly competitive applicants (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.088, 0.09], point estimate: 0.09. Treatment X is genuinely better by 9.0 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=56.2%, Y=70.6% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In less competitive applicants: X=87% > Y=77%. In highly competitive applicants: X=46% > Y=38%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Department A is applied more to highly competitive applicants (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
